coredata.dc:title: SWCNTs@GQDs composites as nanocarriers for enzyme-free dual-signal amplification electrochemical immunoassay of cancer biomarker
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Yanan
Wang, Yuxin
Yan, Hongye
Wu, Yu
Zhu, Chengzhou
Du, Dan
Lin, Yuehe
coredata.dc:description: Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) as a typical biomarker plays a remarkable role in various human physiological processes. A sensitive and rapid method should be developed to realize the precise diagnosis of cancer at its early stage. In this work, we successfully synthesized the peroxidase-like graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and utilized it to fabricate an enzyme-free electrochemical immunosensor towards CEA by forming a single-wall carbon nanotubes@GQDs (SWCNTs@GQDs) composite. In addition to the SWCNTs@GQDs, reduced graphene oxide-Au nanoparticles (rGO-Au NPs) with the huge specific surface area and excellent conductivity were employed to modify the electrodes, leading to a dual-signal amplification result. Both the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and cyclic voltammetry measurements were performed to evaluate the interface properties of the layer-by-layer modified electrodes. The proposed electrochemical immunosensor displayed a good specificity and sensitivity towards the detection of CEA ranging from 50 pg mL−1 to 650 pg mL−1 with a low detection limit of 5.3 pg mL−1. This simple and inexpensive immunosensor shows promising potential for the determination of other biomarkers in clinical translation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Peroxidase-like nanomaterials
Electrochemical immunosensors
Carcinoembryonic antigen
Signal amplification

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Performance assessment of the immunosensor towards CEA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of A) GO and B) rGO-Au NPs. C) TEM image of rGO-Au NPs. D) UV-vis absorption spectrum of GO (a), rGO-AuNPs (b), inset: photograph of rGO-AuNPs (c) and GO (d).

TEM images of A) GQDs. B) SWCNTs. C) GQDs-SWCNTs-Ab2. D) FITR spectrum of GQDs. E) UV-vis absorption of GQDs (a), GQDs, H2O2, TMB, H2SO4 mixtures, inset: photograph of GQDs (c) and (d) GQDs, H2O2, TMB, H2SO4 mixtures.

A) EIS measurements at the electrodes GCE (a), rGO-AuNPs/GCE (b), Ab1/rGO-AuNPs/GCE(c), BSA/Ab1/rGO-AuNPs/GCE (d), CEA/BSA/Ab1/rGO-AuNPs/GCE (e), BSA/CEA/BSA/Ab1/rGO-AuNPs/GCE (f), Ab2-SWCNTs-GQDs/BSA/CEA/BSA/Ab1/rGO-AuNPs/GCE (g). B) CV of GCE(a), rGO-AuNPs/GCE (b), Ab1/rGO-AuNPs/GCE (c), BSA/Ab1/rGO-AuNPs/GCE (d), CEA/BSA/Ab1/rGO-AuNPs/GCE (e), Ab2-SWCNTs-GQDs/BSA/CEA/BSA/Ab1/rGO-AuNPs/GCE (f) All the experiments are at 1 mM K3 [Fe(CN)6]/K4 [Fe(CN)6] solution containing 0.1 M KCl.

CV curves of Ab1/rGO-AuNPs/GCE (a) in pH 7.4 PBS, Ab1/rGO-AuNPs/GCE (b), Ab2-GQDs/CEA/Ab1/rGO-AuNPs/GCE (c), Ab2-SWCNTs-GQDs/CEA/Ab1/rGO-AuNPs/GCE (d) in pH 7.4 PBS containing 2 mM H2O2 and 50 μM thionine.
The optimization of experimental conditions: (A) Incubation time for capturing CEA (a) and binding to Ab2 (b). (B) pH in 2 mM H2O2 and 50 μM thionine.

SWV curves of Ab2-SWCNTs-GQDs/CEA/Ab1/rGO-AuNPs/GCE with concentrations of CEA at 50 (a), 100 (b), 200 (c), 250 (d), 350 (e), 500 (f), 650 (g) pg mL−1 in pH 8.5 PBS containing 50 μM thionine and 2 mM H2O2. Inset: Calibration curves of the immunosensor for detection of CEA.
SWV curves of the immunosensor after incubating with different antigen 50 ng mL−1 CA 125, 50 ng mL-1 Mb, 50 ng mL−1 p53, 0.5 ng mL−1 CEA.
Schematic illustration of the procedure for the fabrication of the immunosensor.
Comparison of the proposed electrochemical immunosensor performance with other reported methods towards CEA.
Recovery rates of the immunosensor for different concentrations of CEA in human serum.

head.ce:title: SWCNTs@GQDs composites as nanocarriers for enzyme-free dual-signal amplification electrochemical immunoassay of cancer biomarker

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Pesticide and Chemical Biology, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry, Central China Normal University, Wuhan, 430079, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Pesticide and Chemical Biology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical and Materials Engineering, Washington State University, Pullman, WA, 99164, United States

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mechanical and Materials Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contribute equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) as a typical biomarker plays a remarkable role in various human physiological processes. A sensitive and rapid method should be developed to realize the precise diagnosis of cancer at its early stage. In this work, we successfully synthesized the peroxidase-like graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and utilized it to fabricate an enzyme-free electrochemical immunosensor towards CEA by forming a single-wall carbon nanotubes@GQDs (SWCNTs@GQDs) composite. In addition to the SWCNTs@GQDs, reduced graphene oxide-Au nanoparticles (rGO-Au NPs) with the huge specific surface area and excellent conductivity were employed to modify the electrodes, leading to a dual-signal amplification result. Both the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and cyclic voltammetry measurements were performed to evaluate the interface properties of the layer-by-layer modified electrodes. The proposed electrochemical immunosensor displayed a good specificity and sensitivity towards the detection of CEA ranging from 50 pg mL−1 to 650 pg mL−1 with a low detection limit of 5.3 pg mL−1. This simple and inexpensive immunosensor shows promising potential for the determination of other biomarkers in clinical translation.

ce:section.ce:para: Biomarkers are kinds of important indicators to reflect the state of biological systems. Implementing diagnosis of diseases at an early stage by precise detection of biomarkers can significantly enhance the cure or survival rates of patients [1]. Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) is a typical tumor biomarker involved in a broad array of cancer, including colon carcinomas [2], ovarian carcinoma [3], breast carcinoma [4], pancreatic carcinoma [5], lung carcinoma [6] and et al. In normal conditions, the level of the sugar-dominating CEA is less than 2.5 ng mL−1 in healthy human blood while that's no more than 5 ng mL−1 in blood of smokers [7]. CEA is also the most frequently used as an indicator in asymptomatic patients and recurrence indicator in patients who received primary therapy [8,9]. As a result, it is of great value to achieve a sensitive and early detection of CEA to monitor the process of cancer in clinical research. Among the various technologies for the detection of biomarkers, electrochemical immunoassay has been proved to be a highly sensitive and inexpensive approach [10]. For example, enzymatic electrochemical sensors based on the redox of H2O2 have made impressive progress. However, horseradish peroxidase (HRP) suffers from several intrinsic shortcomings, such as low stability, environment-sensitive catalytic activity, and high cost, which hamper their applications [11,12]. Thus, much effort has been devoted to exploiting artificial enzymes and nanozymes with high stability to substitute the traditional natural enzymes [13–17].

Zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are classified as graphene derivatives. Due to the photoluminescence property and stronger photostability than semiconductor quantum dots, GQDs are widely applied in biosensing, bioimaging and drug delivery [18,19]. Recently, GQDs are reported to exhibit peroxidase-like activity, which is better than graphene oxide (GO) [20–23]. Since carboxyl group plays a vital role in the peroxidase-like catalytic ability of GO, Qu's group explored the catalytic sites of three GQDs derivatives thoroughly [22]. They claimed that the ketonic carbonyl groups of GQDs dominated the catalytically active sites and the carboxylic groups were major substrate binding sites [24,25]. Due to the similar electrochemical properties to graphene and better nanozyme-based catalytic activity than GO, GQDs has been widely used in electrochemical sensors [18].

Signal amplification plays a crucial role in electrochemical immunoassay to improve sensitivity and obtain low detection limits [26]. To increase the electrocatalytic activity and sensitivity of biosensors, nanomaterials with high catalytic activity and conductivity are usually used to accelerate the signal transduction and provide abundant sites for loading of signal tags [27]. Incorporation of multienzyme-labeling nanocarriers and nanomaterials-modified electrodes is an effective approach to amplify the responsive signal [28]. Carbon nanomaterials have attracted much attention and been extensively employed for signal amplification in biosensors due to their excellent conductivity and huge specific surface area [29–31]. Carbon nanotube (CNT) is one of the most frequently used carbon nanomaterials in the construction of biosensors in virtue of the capability to minimize surface fouling of the sensors and keep strong electrocatalytic activity [32–34]. CNTs are easily endowed with multi-functional groups through flexible surface modification process either by non-covalent or covalent functionalization [35]. Thanks to the rich surface chemistry, CNTs can either be used to modify the electrodes or act as carriers of enzyme [36–38].

In this paper, we reported an enzyme-free electrochemical immunosensor towards the sensitive determination of CEA through the peroxidase-like GQDs catalyzing the reduction of H2O2. The preparation procedure for immunosensor was illustrated in Scheme 1 . Single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) were incorporated with GQDs and secondary antibody (Ab2), forming SWCNTs@GQDs/Ab2 nanocomposites to amplify the responsive current. Moreover, we employed a hybrid of reduced graphene oxide (rGO) and gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) as a modified electrode material to enhance the electrocatalytic properties of carbon electrodes. Taking advantage of the dual signal amplification from rGO-Au NPs and SWCNTs, the immunosensor exhibited a good selectivity and sensitivity to the determination of CEA.

N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), biotin- NHS, avidin, bovine serum albumin (BSA), and 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St.Louis, USA). Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA/CD66e)ELISA kit including CEA capture antibody (Ab1), CEA and CEA detection antibody (Ab2) were purchased from Abcam (Shanghai, China). GO and carboxylated SWCNTs were purchased from XF Nano (Nanjing, China). 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) was purchased from Invitrogen Corporation. HAuCl4·4H2O was purchased from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. H2SO4 (98%), H2O2 (30%), KCl, NaCl, ascorbic acid, Na2HPO4·12H2O, KH2PO4, ethanol (98%) and 2-(N-morpholino)ethanesulfonic acid (MES) were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Ultrapure water with a resistivity higher than 18.2 MΩ cm was used in all experiments (Millipore, Co., USA).

All the electrochemical tests, including electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), cyclic voltammetry (CV) and square wave voltammetry (SWV) were carried out by an electrochemical workstation CHI 660E (CHI, Shanghai) coupled with a three-electrode system. The glassy carbon electrode (GCE) (3 mm) were purchased from CHI. The electrochemical measurements were carried out a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) filled with saturated KCl aqueous solution and Pt wire were used as reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were obtained with an H-7000FA microscope and JSM-6700F microscope, respectively. The characterization of characteristic functional groups of nanomaterials was performed by a Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer (Bruker, Tensor 27). The UV-vis absorption spectrums were collected by a UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan).

The rGO-Au NPs composites were fabricated following the previously reported one-pot chemical reduction method [39]. Briefly, a mixture of 80 μL 5 mM HAuCl4·4H2O and 20 μL 1.0 g L−1 GO were diluted with 800 μL ultrapure water. Then, a volume of 100 μL 0.1 M ascorbic acid solution was injected into the mixture under the sonication within 20 min. The mixture was aged for another 48 h and purified by centrifugation to obtain the product. The obtained rGO-Au NPs composites were redispersed in ultrapure water.

A process of pyrolyzing l-glutamic acid was proceeded to synthesize GQDs [23]. 2.0 g l-glutamic acid was added to a round-bottomed flask, then heated the temperature to 210 °C with a heating mantle. The powder melt to colorless liquid, then turned to brown yellow, suggesting the formation of GQDs. Subsequently, 10 mL of ultrapure water was injected to the mixture and kept stirring for 30 min. After the solution cooling down to room temperature, collecting the supernatant by centrifugation at 10000 rpm and stored the resultant GQDs in dark.

1.5 mg of carboxyl group functionalized SWCNTs were dispersed in 2 mL pH 7.0 phosphate buffer solution (PBS), and sonicated for 10 min to form a homogeneous liquid. Then, 1 mL of pH 6.0 MES buffer containing 400 mM EDC and 100 mM NHS was added to activate the carboxyl group of SWCNTs by shaking 15 min at room temperature. The activated SWCNTs were collected by centrifuging at 10000 rpm for 10 min, and rinsed with PBS to remove the unreacted EDC and NHS. For the synthesis of Ab2-SWCNTs-GQDs conjugates, 10 μL of Ab2 and 500 μL of 15 mg mL−1 GQDs were added to the activated SWCNTs and shaken overnight. The precipitations were separated by centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 10 min to remove the unconjugated Ab2 and GQDs, followed by rinsing with PBS for four times. The purified Ab2-SWCNTs-GQDs conjugates were dispersed in 1 mL of PBS containing 0.05% Tween-20 and stored in 4 °C refrigerator. To eliminate the aggregation of Ab2-SWCNTs-GQDs, the supernatant of stock solution should be discarded and diluted 10 times with PBS containing 0.05 (w/v) % Tween-20 before the immunosensing experiments. The Ab2-SWCNTs-GQDs conjugates are stable and can provide a reproducible signal for at least 7 days.

Prior to surface modification, the GCE was polished with 1.0,0.3 and 0.05 μm alumina powder successively, followed by rinsing with deionized water and sonicating with acetone, ethanol and deionized water, respectively.

10 μL of rGO-Au NPs composites, 10 μL of 0.2% chitosan solution was added onto GCE, respectively, each step followed by an air-drying process. Then the GCE was incubated with 10 μL of 2.5% glutaraldehyde for 2 h, followed by washing the GCE with water and adding 10 μL of Ab1. The GCE was incubated for another 60 min and then aged overnight in 4 °C refrigerator. The modified GCE was rinsed with PBST (PBS containing 0.05% Tween-20) and PBS for three times, respectively, and further incubated with 10 μL of 3% BSA for 1 h to block the non-specific binding sites. The excess BSA was removed by washing with PBST and PBS, each for three times. The CEA was then added onto the GCE and incubated for 50 min, followed by repeating the washing and blocking steps. Afterward, 10 μL of Ab2-SWCNTs-GQDs conjugates was dropped onto the GCE and incubated for 1 h, followed by rinsing with PBST and PBS. The modified GCE was stored in 4 °C refrigerator prior to use.

A sandwich immunoassay was utilized for the determination of CEA. The Ab1/rGO-AuNPs/GCE was treated with 10 μL of CEA at a gradient concentration. The CV measurement of the immunosensor was carried out in PBS containing 2 mM H2O2 and 50 μM thionine.

The rGO-Au NPs composites were synthesized by a one-step reduction method with ascorbic acid. Oxygen-containing groups of GO play important role in the growth process of Au NPs because of their strong affinity to Au3+ [40]. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the SEM image of GO displays a typical wrinkle morphology. Upon addition of ascorbic acid, Au3+ absorbed on GO and GO were reduced to Au NP and rGO, respectively, forming the rGO-Au NPs composites. The mass ratio of Au NPs to rGO is about 4:1. Fig. 1B and C shows the SEM and TEM images of rGO-Au NPs composites. It is clear that the as-prepared Au NPs distribute on the rGO nanosheets uniformly. The UV-vis absorption spectrum further verified the successful preparation of rGO-Au NPs composites. As shown in Fig. 1D, GO exhibits a characteristic absorbance at 230 nm (curve a), which is due to the π-π* transitions from the aromatic C=C bond [41]. After GO reduction, the peak has a red shift to 285 nm, and another peak appears at the wavelength of 520 nm (curve b), which is attributed to the absorption of Au NPs. The inset in Fig. 1D also shows a significant change of color from brown-yellow to pink due to the formation of rGO-Au NPs composites.

The as-synthesized GQDs were first characterized by high-resolution TEM. Fig. 2 A shows that the obtained GQDs display a uniform dot-like morphology with a size of approximately 3–5 nm. The inset shows the high crystallinity of the GQDs, and lattice spacing is accurately measured to be 0.250 nm. Besides, the GQDs exhibits a strong blue fluorescence under the irradiation of UV light at a wavelength of 365 nm and a pronounced emission at 440 nm with the excitation wavelength of 360 nm (Figure SI-1). The surface characteristic groups of the GQDs were verified by the FITR spectrum. Fig. 2D shows obvious stretching vibration at 1420 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1, which is attributed to the C=C bond of graphite and indicates the formation of GQDs. The strong absorbance at 1720 cm−1 was due to the classical stretching vibration of C=O bond. The broad peaks ranging from 2500 to 2800 cm−1 came from the O–H bond stretching of carboxylic acid. The peak at 3320 cm−1 was due to the N-H bond stretching vibration of amine. These results indicated that the GQDs possessed abundant amino and carboxyl groups, which provided the accessible conjugated sites for the formation of composites.

To assess the capability of GQDs to catalyze H2O2, TMB was used as an indicator by observing the absorption and color change. As shown in Fig. 2E, the GQDs shows characteristic absorption at 230 nm and 320 nm (curve a), respectively. After incubating the GQDs with a mixture of H2O2 and TMB in pH 5.0 acetate buffer solution for 2 min, the color of the solution turned to blue from light yellow quickly (data not shown). Then the color turned to bright yellow by the quenching of 1.0 M H2SO4 (inset). Also, an obvious absorption peak appearing at 450 nm was due to the oxidation of TMB (curve b).

Conjugation of Ab2 and GQDs to SWCNTs was achieved by the formation of amide bonds between the –NH2 of Ab2 or GQDs and –COOH of SWCNTs. As can be seen from Fig. 2B and SI-2, the pristine SWCNTs are intertwined and have poor dispersity. Fig. 2C shows that the SWCNTs are coated by GQDs to form a composite structure, indicating the successful formation of Ab2-SWCNTs-GQDs conjugates.

EIS is an effective technique for providing the surface information of the electron transfer rate of electrodes. The Nyquist plots of EIS conclude two parts, namely the high frequencies and the low frequencies. The diameter of semicircle represents the charge-transfer resistance (Rct) and the linear corresponds to electrochemical behavior limited by diffusion. To explore the signal amplification process of the modified electrodes, both the EIS and CV measurements have been carried out. As shown in Fig. 3 A, curve a represents the Rct of bare GCE, which is very small. The semicircle diameter decreases when rGO-AuNPs is dropped onto the electrode (curve b), indicates that the rGO-AuNPs has a high electron transfer rate. An obvious increase of Rct was observed after capturing the Ab1 onto the electrode (curve c). Then, the value of Rct significantly increases from curve d to f, with the immobilization of BSA, CEA, BSA in sequence. The increasing protein molecules hindered electron transfer, indicating the successful modification of electrode. Afterward, the Rct of electrode decreases slightly after incubating Ab2-SWCNTs-GQDs (curve g), possibly due to the introduction of SWCNTs with good conductivity. According to the variation of Rct, it is believable that the proposed immunosensor has been fabricated successfully.

To explore the interface properties of electrodes, CV measurements were implemented with a scan rate at 50 mV s−1 and scanning potential in a range of −0.2 V to 0.7 V in 1 mM K3 [Fe(CN)6]/K4 [Fe(CN)6] solution containing 0.1 M KCl. As can be seen in Fig. 3B, the redox peak current significantly increases when the rGO-AuNPs (curve b) with great electrical conductivity is coated onto the bare GCE (curve a). However, the current tends to decrease gradually when the Ab1, BSA, CEA are immobilized onto the electrode in sequence (curve c to e). Similar to the results of EIS, the decreases of current were due to the impediment of proteins towards electron transfer. Thanks to the good conductivity of SWCNTs, the responsive current increases after incubating the modified electrode with Ab2-SWCNTs-GQDs. The current changes of CV illustrated the successful fabrication of immunosensor, which agreed well with EIS results mentioned above.

Having verified the excellent improvement of interface properties by rGO-AuNPs and SWCNTs, the performance of the immunosensor was further evaluated by recording the CV curves in H2O2 and thionine solutions. As shown in Fig. 4 , the CV curve at Ab1/rGO-AuNPs/GCE (curve a) show no significant signal in pH 7.4 PBS. But a pair of stable and well-shaped redox peaks at −0.142 V and −0.205 V were obtained while recording the CV curve at Ab1/rGO-AuNPs/GCE in PBS containing 2 mM H2O2 and 50 μM thionine (curve b). They correspond to the electrochemical oxidation and reduction peaks of thionine, respectively. No significant change was observed after binding the CEA (data not shown). However, the reduction current of thionine remarkably increased (curve c) after incubating the electrode CEA/Ab1/rGO-AuNPs/GCE with Ab2-GQDs composites because of the enhanced catalytic reaction by GQDs. As expected, further increase of the reduction current was achieved at Ab2-SWCNTs-GQDs/CEA/Ab1/rGO-AuNPs/GCE due to the signal amplification caused by SWCNTs (curve d).

The incubation time of the specific recognition between Ab1 and CEA or CEA and Ab2 have a vital influence on immunoassay results. A series of experiments were performed by changing the incubation time from 10 min to 70 min at an interval of 10 min. As illustrated in Fig. 5 A, the responsive current increases with increasing incubation time of CEA (curve a). Then it approaches a plateau at the time point of 50 min. As for the incubation time of Ab2-SWCNTs-GQDs, a steady value of responsive current was achieved after 60 min (curve b). Therefore, the optimal incubation time of CEA and Ab2-SWCNTs-GQDs are set as 50 min and 60 min for the following experiments, respectively.

The pH of PBS is another important parameter for the electrochemical signal. The responsive currents of the immunosensor were investigated in the pH range of 5.0–9.0. Fig. 5B shows that the responsive current of the immunosensor rises with an increase in pH, and tends to a plateau at pH 8.5. Considering that the peracid or overbase environment can affect the activity of protein, such as the Ab1 and CEA, PBS at pH 8.5 was chosen as the optimal pH value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance assessment of the immunosensor towards CEA
ce:section.ce:para: The specificity of the electrochemical immunosensor was then investigated to confirm the reliability of the proposed immunosensor. Three different kinds of antigen including the ovarian cancer antigen (CA125), human myoglobin (Mb) and phosphorylated protein (p53) were utilized as negative control groups. The SWV responsive current of each group was measured in the same experimental condition with CEA. It is clear that the responsive current of CEA is much larger than that of any other group, indicating the good specificity of the immunosensor towards CEA (Fig. 7 ).

To validate the feasibility of the proposed immunosensor applied in real sample assay, the immunosensor was tested in human serum matrix. In this work, three different concentrations of CEA (100, 250, 325 pg mL−1) were dissolved in 20-fold diluted human serum, followed by determining the CEA concentrations of each group. The recovery rates of each group were calculated to be 103.6%, 103% and 91.0%, respectively (Table 2 ). The results suggested the designed immunosensor holding great potential in the clinical use for the CEA detection in serum samples.

In this work, we have successfully synthesized the peroxidase-like GQDs and the GQDs shows strongly catalytic reaction towards H2O2. An electrochemical immunosensor towards CEA in a combination of the synergistic signal amplification of both rGO-AuNPs and SWCNTs-GQDs is designed. The designed immunosensor exhibits high sensitivity for the determination of CEA ranging from 50 pg mL−1 to 650 pg mL−1 with a low detection limit of 5.3 pg mL−1. Besides, this immunosensor has excellent specificity towards CEA and holds great potential in the clinical use for the CEA detection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support from the Recruitment Program of Global Youth Experts. This work was also supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21575047).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2018.08.023.
